content.assessment: A statutory delay to age 14+ interacts directly with cohort and class-size mechanics, since a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase would, by removing the 6/8-year entry-point fragmentation, allow larger consolidated cohorts to more easily meet the 26-34 statutory class-size band than fragmented multi-track pipelines can in shrinking localities. This is a genuine demographic strength: consolidation of tracks map onto consolidation of viable class sizes. However, the proposal's Finnish/Polish comparative evidence base does not engage with Hungary's specific spatial unevenness (F-live-demography-04) or the already-severe rural viability crisis independent of national cohort decline; a national comprehensive-phase mandate could still face acute local-capacity mismatches if transition phasing (which the proposal flags as a Polish-derived risk) does not explicitly model regional cohort trajectories. The 20+ year embedded nature of Hungary's decline (F-live-demography-02) means the planning horizon for such a structural reform must be multi-decade, which the proposal's legislative-amendment pathway could accommodate but does not explicitly address.
opportunities: Comprehensive-phase model could be phased regionally, prioritizing areas already showing severe contraction (F-live-demography-04) where consolidation is most urgently needed
strengths: Comprehensive-phase consolidation directly addresses the mechanical class-size viability problem (F-live-demography-05) better than a multi-track model, since larger unified cohorts are easier to staff to statutory minimums
Long legislative pathway and bundling requirement create room for a multi-decade planning horizon appropriate to a 20+ year structural demographic trend (F-live-demography-02)
threats: Without addressing the aging teacher workforce and rural shortages (F-live-demography-09), a bundled reform requiring new curriculum and teacher training may be undeliverable precisely in the regions where demographic pressure is most acute
weaknesses: Proposal's own risk analysis (Polish transition-shortfall precedent) does not explicitly incorporate Hungary's already-documented severe regional unevenness in cohort contraction (F-live-demography-04), risking a repeat of capacity-shortfall dynamics in specific fragile regions during any transition

Aging, rural-shortage-prone teacher workforce (F-live-demography-09) is not addressed as a precondition for successful bundled curriculum/teacher-training reform, despite the Finnish model explicitly requiring teacher-training change
